user.name: DAIR.AI
user.description: Democratizing AI research, education, and technologies. New AI learning portal: https://t.co/LRnpZN7L4c

fullText:
System scaling is the next real bottleneck in agentic AI.

If you build agent orchestration layers, this is a clean map of where the engineering leverage actually sits. The labs own the model. You own the harness, and that is increasingly where agent quality is won or lost.

The default mental model still puts all the weight on the foundation model. Bigger model, better agent. But agent behavior actually emerges from the whole stack around it. Memory substrate, context constructor, skill routing, orchestration loop, and the verification and governance layer.

This new research calls that stack the harness and argues we should treat it as a first-class object of design and evaluation. It names three core bottlenecks to scale. Context governance, trustworthy memory, and dynamic skill routing. It also ships CheetahClaws, a Python-native reference harness, and compares it with Claude Code and OpenClaw.

Paper: https://t.co/HynpWFVUqq

Learn to build effective AI agents in our academy: https://t.co/LRnpZN7L4c

user.name: Vincent Boucher · AGI.eth → ASI.eth
user.description: President, @Quebec_AI & @Montreal_AI. AGI.eth → ASI.eth. Sovereign Intelligence. Institutional Command. Govern capability before capability governs.

fullText:
One of the most important objects in agentic AI may turn out to be a Markdown file.

Not the model weights.
Not the prompt.

The skill document.

A new Microsoft paper introduces SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills.

The thesis is sharp:

If an agent’s procedure is what needs to adapt, then the procedure itself should be trainable.

A skill is portable procedural memory:

how to gather evidence
how to call tools
how to format outputs
how to follow domain conventions
how to avoid failure modes

Today, most skills are hand-written, generated once, or revised through loose self-reflection.

SkillOpt treats the skill as the external state of a frozen agent and optimizes it in text space with the discipline of deep learning.

The analogy is not cosmetic.

Rollouts become batches.
Failures become update signals.
Add/delete/replace edits become parameter updates.
The edit budget becomes a textual learning rate.
The held-out gate becomes validation.
Rejected edits become negative feedback.
The slow/meta update carries stable lessons across epochs.

The deployed artifact is just a compact best_skill.md file.

No weight updates.
No extra inference-time model calls.
No prompt rewrite accepted on vibes.

Every edit must improve held-out validation performance.

That is the shift:

from prompt engineering
to skill training

from ad hoc revision
to controlled text-space optimization

The results are hard to ignore.

Across six benchmarks, seven target models, and three execution harnesses — direct chat, Codex, and Claude Code — SkillOpt is best or tied on all 52 evaluated cells.

On GPT-5.5, it improves no-skill accuracy by:

+23.5 points in direct chat
+24.8 inside the Codex agentic loop
+19.1 inside Claude Code

The transfer results may be even more important: optimized skills retain value across model scales, across Codex and Claude Code environments, and even on a nearby math benchmark without further optimization.

That matters.

The future of agents may not only be about retraining models.

It may be about training the procedures around them.

Portable.
Auditable.
Reusable.
Model-agnostic.

SkillOpt suggests a new layer in the agent stack:

external optimizer-shaped memory.

Not weights.
Not prompts.
Operational knowledge, trained.

Full credit to the authors:
Yifan Yang, Ziyang Gong, Weiquan Huang, Qihao Yang, Ziwei Zhou, Zisu Huang, Yan Li, Xuemei Gao, Qi Dai, Bei Liu, Kai Qiu, Yuqing Yang, Dongdong Chen, Xue Yang, Chong Luo.

Paper:
https://t.co/Cqael2lmYq

Code:
https://t.co/pfsZWi8wMC

I’m attaching the first page because the abstract is worth reading closely.

The next generation of agents may not only learn what to answer.

They may learn how to operate.

#AIResearch #Agents #LLM

fullText:
Introducing Project Luxo: a new initiative exploring how AI-generated video has crossed the uncanny valley.

Over the past few weeks, we’ve shared a series of short films with Hollywood executives, producers, directors, writers, actors and reporters. The response has been unanimous: the technology is finally good enough to disappear behind the story.

One of those films is The Rogue — a fully AI-generated, ten-minute film that spent over a decade unproduced in the traditional system: too expensive, too difficult, too risky. Created with Runway in under a month by a single person.

Learn more about The Rogue and Project Luxo at the link below.

user.description: Building @dair_ai • Prev: Meta AI, Elastic, PhD • New AI learning portal: https://t.co/1e8RZKs4uX

fullText:
New research from Microsoft Research

I see a lot of AI engineers handwriting agent skill docs and hope they generalize.

Probably not optimal. This works show why.

It treats the skill doc as a trainable external state of a frozen agent instead.

It introduces SkillOpt, where an optimizer model makes validation-gated edits to the skill file. It adds, deletes, or replaces instructions, with a textual learning rate that controls how aggressively each round rewrites the doc. The agent itself never changes.

SkillOpt is best or tied on all 52 (model, benchmark, harness) cells.

On GPT-5.5 it adds 23.5 points in direct chat, 24.8 with Codex, and 19.1 with Claude Code over no skill. It beats human-written skills, TextGrad, GEPA, and EvoSkill, carries zero extra inference-time cost, and the learned skills transfer across models and harnesses.

Paper: https://t.co/mNgTmmT32U

Learn to build effective AI agents in our academy: https://t.co/1e8RZKs4uX

fullText:
Inflation since 1971 when gold and $ decoupled: today, $1T buys ~4% of all the gold ever mined (ATGEM). In 1971, only $5B were needed. In that sense, a trillionaire today is like a 5-billionaire of 1971, a billionaire like a 5-millionaire, a millionaire like a 5-thousandaire.

Since 1971, ATGEM has roughly doubled. So has the world's population. Gold per human: still roughly 1 ounce (~28g). 

Gold's purchasing power has remained largely unchanged since Roman times, whereas the value of all fiat currencies since then has dropped to zero or almost zero. 

As of 2026, ATGEM would fit into a cube with sides measuring ~22 meters.

Rough estimates of historical market caps of major companies as fractions of ATGEM:

★ 1971: 1 ounce of gold = $35;  IBM market cap $37B ~1/3 of ATGEM

★ 2007: gold $800; Petrochina 1st public $1T company ~1/4 of ATGEM

★ 2010: gold $1100; Saudi Aramco Financial Times estimate $7T > 90% of ATGEM

★ 2019: gold $1560; Aramco after IPO $2T ~1/5 of ATGEM

★ 2022: gold $1800; Apple 1st public $3T company ~1/4 of ATGEM

★ Jul 2025: gold $3300; NVIDIA 1st public $4T company ~1/5 of ATGEM

★ Oct 2025: gold $4000; NVIDIA 1st public $5T company ~1/5 of ATGEM

Massive inflation is the only reason we are talking about trillions now. Recall the 1923 hyperinflation in the Weimar Republic when everyone became a multi-trillionaire.
